question_a.banner_statement: Yishun is better framed as a value town than as the outright cheapest town in Singapore.
question_a.hypothesis: After controlling for flat type and age, Yishun flats are among the cheaper resale options.
question_a.method: Controlled price-per-sqm comparison within flat-type and age bands, plus log(price-per-sqm) regression with year controls.
question_a.interpretation: Yishun remains cheaper than the peer median after matching on flat type and age.
chart_commentary: Start with the box plots to show which towns look cheapest when we focus on the three most common flat types.
Then use the simple coefficient chart to show the adjusted regression result after controlling for flat type, age, and year.
Add the all-town coefficient chart to compare the adjusted town ranking directly across the full market.
Use the price-versus-space scatter to explain why buyers can experience Yishun as good value even when total prices are not the absolute lowest.
End with the interaction chart to show whether the target-town discount is consistent across flat types or concentrated in a few segments.
limitations: Town fixed effects still absorb other location amenities beyond the controlled variables.
Band-level comparisons simplify age into buckets and do not fully equalize every unit characteristic.

flat_sizes.banner_statement: Newer completions tend to be smaller in larger flat types, but the pattern is not universal across all flat categories.
flat_sizes.hypothesis: If newer HDB completions are designed smaller, then average floor area should decline with completion year within flat type.
flat_sizes.method: Track floor area by completion year, compare within flat type, and estimate a controlled completion-year trend with town controls.
flat_sizes.interpretation: The completion-year view points to a real design-size decline in larger flat types, especially 4-room, 5-room, and Executive flats.
chart_commentary: Start with the connected-scatter chart by completion year to frame the question as a design trend rather than a transaction-mix trend.
Use the slope chart to identify which flat types show the strongest decline as completion year becomes newer.
Use the recent-completion chart as the clearest visual for the presentation, because the decline is easiest to see there.
Use the year-dummy interaction chart on the method slide to show a non-linear adjusted completion-year profile by flat type after controlling for town.
limitations: Completion year is derived from the available lease-commence information and may not equal the exact physical completion date in every case.
Resale transactions may not reflect the full underlying stock of flats built in each completion year.

dtl2.banner_statement: Near-station DTL2 buildings saw a post-opening uplift, but the pre-trend evidence still means the effect should be presented cautiously.
dtl2.hypothesis: Downtown Line Stage 2 increased resale prices for buildings closer to DTL2 stations relative to farther buildings in the same corridor towns.
dtl2.method: Building-level difference-in-differences using distance to DTL2 stations, plus an event-study pre-trend check.
dtl2.control_definition: Distance to DTL2 station in (1.5, 4.0] km
dtl2.interpretation: Near-station buildings show a positive post-opening uplift, but the lead coefficients are jointly significant before opening, so the causal claim must be qualified.
chart_commentary: Lead with the near-versus-far building chart because it makes the building-level treatment definition concrete.
Use the event-study coefficients to show both the post-opening uplift and the statistically significant pre-trend leads.
Use the distance-band chart as supporting intuition for how the price-per-sqm gradient changes across the corridor.
Use the treatment map to show exactly how the 1km exposure region is defined around the DTL2 stations.
limitations: The lead coefficients indicate that near-station buildings were already on a different path before 2016.
Distance bands improve exposure measurement, but they still do not fully control for project-level amenity differences near stations.

coe.banner_statement: The key issue is differential COE sensitivity, not simple co-movement. A positive far-town interaction is consistent with a housing-car substitution story, but the sign is not guaranteed by the prompt itself.
coe.hypothesis: If buyers substitute toward cheaper far-out flats to preserve a housing-plus-car budget when COE rises, Sengkang and Punggol should show a more positive COE response than central towns. If higher COE mainly suppresses car demand, the interaction could instead be negative.
coe.method: Monthly town-level HDB resale panel comparing Sengkang and Punggol against central towns after merging with official COE premiums from Singapore data.gov.sg.
coe.interpretation: Sengkang and Punggol appear modestly more COE-sensitive than central towns. Because the estimated interaction is positive, the result is directionally consistent with a housing-car substitution story, but it remains correlational and should not be presented as proof of household-level car-buying behavior.
chart_commentary: Open with the indexed chart only as intuition: far-town prices and COE can appear to move together over time, but co-movement alone does not identify the mechanism.
Then explain that the real test is differential sensitivity versus central towns, and that the sign is theoretically ambiguous unless we explicitly assume a housing-car substitution channel.
Use the far-town-versus-central spread chart to show the relative comparison that actually matters for the claim.

Treat the partial-regression chart as the controlled comparison after town and month effects are removed, and explain that a positive interaction supports substitution while a negative one would support a pure affordability squeeze.
limitations: This is a town-level association, not direct evidence that individual households used housing savings to buy cars.
Sengkang and Punggol also changed in many other ways over the sample period, including amenities, transport access, and housing mix.
Other macro factors can still affect both COE prices and housing demand even with month fixed effects.
